past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed 3 years ago, not on anticoagulation due to patient refusal after a fall with minor scalp laceration
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago
Remote history of peptic ulcer disease, resolved

medications.indication: Atrial fibrillation rate control and hypertension

family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 65
Mother had a stroke at age 78, survived with residual left-sided weakness
Brother with hypertension and type 2 diabetes

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 30 pack-year history, quit 10 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in single-story home

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but distressed. Oriented to self only. Global aphasia: unable to name objects, follow complex commands, or repeat phrases. Can follow simple one-step commands with the left hand inconsistently. No meaningful verbal output aside from stereotypic utterances ('tan-tan'). Unable to read or write.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Right gaze preference with inability to cross midline to the left. Left lower facial droop, sparing the forehead. Gag reflex intact bilaterally. Tongue deviates to the left on protrusion.
neurological_exam.motor: Left upper extremity: flaccid plegia, 0/5 strength throughout. Left lower extremity: 1/5 hip flexion, 0/5 at ankle. Right upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. Pronator drift: left arm falls immediately to bed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased response to pinprick on the left face, arm, and leg. Left-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation. Proprioception absent in left hand.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 3+, left lower extremity 3+ with upgoing plantar response (positive Babinski sign on the left). Right plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess on left side due to plegia. Right finger-to-nose intact without dysmetria.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate due to left hemiplegia.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 18. Left hemineglect present on visual field testing and line bisection. No meningismus.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and inability to speak
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Harold Jennings is a 72-year-old right-handed man with a history of atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia who was brought to the emergency department by EMS after his wife found him slumped in his recliner at approximately 14:30 today. She reports that they had eaten lunch together at 12:15 and he was entirely at his baseline at that time, conversant and eating without difficulty with both hands. She went to run errands and returned home at 14:25, at which point she found him leaning to the right in his chair with his left arm hanging limply at his side. He appeared to be trying to speak but could only produce unintelligible sounds. She immediately called 911. EMS arrived at 14:38 and noted right gaze deviation, left facial droop, left-sided hemiplegia, and global aphasia. A prehospital stroke alert was activated. Finger-stick glucose in the field was 168 mg/dL. He was transported emergently to our comprehensive stroke center. On arrival at 15:02, the patient was in atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response on the cardiac monitor. He appeared anxious and was making repetitive vocalizations but no recognizable words. There was no reported preceding headache, seizure activity, recent head trauma, or febrile illness. His wife confirms he has not been taking any blood thinners. She notes he has been generally well recently with no recent hospitalizations or illness. His last known well time is established at 12:15 today, approximately 2 hours and 47 minutes prior to ED arrival.

findings.location: Left middle cerebral artery territory involving the left insular cortex, left frontal operculum, left temporal operculum, and left parietal lobe
findings.size: Approximately 5.2 x 3.8 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity suggesting onset greater than 3-4 hours
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in left MCA territory

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the left lateral ventricle and subtle leftward sulcal effacement. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Well-defined margins conforming to left MCA vascular territory

signal_characteristics.MRA: Abrupt cutoff of the left MCA at the M1 segment with absent distal flow signal
signal_characteristics.TOF: Loss of flow-related enhancement in the left MCA

additional_observations: Scattered periventricular and subcortical white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2)
Right MCA and bilateral ACA and PCA are patent on MRA
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss appropriate for age
mri.impression: 1. Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion involving the left insular cortex, frontal and temporal opercula, and parietal lobe. 2. Left MCA M1 segment occlusion on MRA. 3. No hemorrhagic transformation. 4. Chronic small vessel ischemic disease. Clinical correlation for IV thrombolysis and/or mechanical thrombectomy is recommended given large vessel occlusion.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic infarction, left MCA territory
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction with ADC confirmation in a vascular territory distribution with corresponding M1 occlusion on MRA
No evidence of intraparenchymal hemorrhage on any sequence

recommended_actions: Urgent neurology and neurointerventional consultation for consideration of IV tPA and mechanical thrombectomy
CT angiography of head and neck for further vascular characterization if not yet obtained
Follow-up imaging in 24 hours to assess for hemorrhagic transformation

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 > 35 mm)
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads
No acute ST elevation or depression suggesting ischemia
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 114 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. Nonspecific ST-T wave changes. No acute ischemic changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Atrial fibrillation is a major risk factor for cardioembolic stroke. The rapid ventricular response warrants rate control. Left ventricular hypertrophy is consistent with longstanding hypertension.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild leukocytosis, likely stress response in acute stroke setting

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely prerenal from suboptimal hydration
Elevated, consistent with known diabetes and acute stress hyperglycemia

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Normal INR confirms patient is not anticoagulated

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Elevated despite statin therapy, contributing cardiovascular risk factor
Significantly above target for patient with multiple vascular risk factors
Low HDL, additional cardiovascular risk factor

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, may represent demand ischemia in setting of AF with RVR and acute stroke
Suboptimally controlled diabetes, significant stroke risk factor

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results reveal mild stress leukocytosis, hyperglycemia consistent with known diabetes and acute stress response, mildly elevated BUN suggesting mild dehydration, suboptimal lipid panel despite statin therapy, and mildly elevated troponin likely representing demand ischemia. Coagulation studies are normal, confirming the patient is not anticoagulated. HbA1c of 7.8% indicates suboptimally controlled diabetes.

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery terminus and proximal left MCA M1 segment

signal_characteristics.CTA: Complete occlusion of the left MCA at the M1 segment origin with thrombus extending into the left ICA terminus (T-occlusion pattern)
Calcified and mixed plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations. Right ICA with approximately 40% stenosis. Left ICA with approximately 50% stenosis proximal to the occlusion.

additional_observations: Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with normal caliber
Basilar artery is patent without stenosis
Circle of Willis demonstrates a fetal-type left PCA
No evidence of dissection in cervical vessels
output.impression: 1. Left MCA M1 occlusion with extension to the left ICA terminus (T-type occlusion), consistent with large vessel occlusion amenable to mechanical thrombectomy. 2. Moderate bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerotic disease. 3. No cervical vessel dissection.
recommended_actions: Consider IV alteplase as bridge to thrombectomy if within time window
CT perfusion to assess penumbra if treatment decision requires further data

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Moderately dilated left atrium, consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation
May be functional due to atrial dilation
TEE recommended if thrombus evaluation needed

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 50%), concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with chronic hypertension, and moderate left atrial dilation consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation. Mild mitral regurgitation present. No intracardiac thrombus visualized, though the left atrial appendage was not well visualized; TEE may be considered for more definitive evaluation. Overall findings strongly support a cardioembolic source in the setting of atrial fibrillation with structural heart disease.

findings: Continuous atrial fibrillation throughout the 24-hour recording period
No periods of sinus rhythm identified
Rare premature ventricular complexes (42 in 24 hours, <1% burden)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
output.interpretation: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals persistent atrial fibrillation with adequate rate control on current medications (average rate 98 bpm). No pauses or ventricular arrhythmias of concern.
output.clinical_correlation: Persistent rather than paroxysmal atrial fibrillation confirmed. This underscores the indication for systemic anticoagulation for secondary stroke prevention once acute phase management is complete.

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel is entirely within normal limits. No hereditary or acquired thrombophilia identified. The stroke etiology is most likely cardioembolic given the known atrial fibrillation and the absence of a hypercoagulable state.

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries demonstrate normal flow velocities
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with antegrade flow
Heterogeneous, predominantly calcified plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations
output.impression: 1. Moderate (50-69%) stenosis of the left internal carotid artery. 2. Mild (<50%) stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. 3. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. The left carotid stenosis, while moderate, is likely a contributing factor but the primary mechanism of this stroke appears cardioembolic given concomitant atrial fibrillation and the MCA M1 occlusion pattern.
recommended_actions: Medical optimization of atherosclerotic risk factors
Consider repeat carotid imaging in 6 months to monitor for progression
Anticoagulation takes priority over antiplatelet therapy given AF-related cardioembolic mechanism

findings.size: Core infarct volume approximately 28 mL; penumbral tissue approximately 85 mL; mismatch ratio 4.0

signal_characteristics.CBF: Severely reduced in left MCA territory core
signal_characteristics.CBV: Reduced in core, relatively preserved in penumbral region
signal_characteristics.Tmax: Tmax >6s volume approximately 113 mL; Tmax >10s volume approximately 35 mL

additional_observations: Core infarct volume below 70 mL threshold
output.impression: CT perfusion demonstrates a favorable target mismatch profile with a relatively small ischemic core (28 mL) and substantial penumbral tissue at risk (85 mL). The mismatch ratio of 4.0 exceeds the threshold of 1.8, supporting candidacy for mechanical thrombectomy per DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria.
recommended_actions: Patient meets imaging criteria for mechanical thrombectomy
Time-sensitive intervention recommended to salvage penumbral tissue

output.query: acute ischemic stroke IV alteplase thrombolysis guidelines 2024

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) recommended within 4.5 hours of symptom onset. 10% given as bolus, remainder over 60 minutes.

results.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke: AHA/ASA Focused Update
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: Mechanical thrombectomy recommended for LVO in anterior circulation within 24 hours if target mismatch profile present on perfusion imaging.

results.title: Tenecteplase versus Alteplase before Thrombectomy for Ischemic Stroke (AcT Trial)
results.key_finding: Tenecteplase non-inferior to alteplase when given before thrombectomy, with simpler single-bolus administration.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) within 4.5 hours of last known well time for eligible acute ischemic stroke patients. For patients with large vessel occlusion, mechanical thrombectomy is strongly recommended and can be performed up to 24 hours from onset if favorable perfusion imaging profile exists. IV thrombolysis should not delay thrombectomy. Tenecteplase is emerging as an alternative to alteplase, particularly in the bridging-to-thrombectomy setting.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left middle cerebral artery territory, cardioembolic etiology

differential.key_distinguishing: CT/MRI shows no hemorrhage; DWI restriction in vascular territory confirms ischemic rather than hemorrhagic etiology
No witnessed seizure activity, no history of epilepsy, persistent deficits without improvement, and imaging confirms acute infarction
Patient age 72 with no migraine history; acute onset with persistent deficits and imaging confirmation of infarction makes this diagnosis untenable
Prehospital glucose was 168 mg/dL and ED glucose was 186 mg/dL; hypoglycemia is excluded

differential.diagnosis: Brain tumor with acute symptom onset
differential.key_distinguishing: MRI shows vascular territory DWI restriction without enhancing mass; acute onset pattern inconsistent with tumor

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and perform rapid triage including last known well time, NIHSS assessment, and fingerstick glucose
Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to rule out hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hemorrhage, possible early ischemic changes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and troponin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal coagulation confirming patient not anticoagulated, mild stress hyperglycemia
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response confirming cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Administer IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) with 10% as bolus and remainder over 60 minutes, given patient is within 4.5 hour window
Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion and plan for thrombectomy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left MCA M1 occlusion with ICA terminus involvement

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurointerventional surgery for mechanical thrombectomy given large vessel occlusion
Obtain brain MRI with DWI to confirm infarct extent and assess for hemorrhagic transformation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion

optimal_actions.action: Manage blood pressure permissively (allow up to 185/110 pre-tPA, then <180/105 for 24h post-tPA)

Initiate anticoagulation with direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban) for secondary stroke prevention once acute phase stable and hemorrhagic transformation excluded, typically 4-14 days post-stroke depending on infarct size
Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate cardiac structure and function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dilated left atrium, LVH, mildly reduced EF consistent with AF-related cardiomyopathy

optimal_actions.action: Admit to neurological ICU or stroke unit for close monitoring

critical_actions: Establish last known well time to determine thrombolysis eligibility
Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage before thrombolysis
Administer IV alteplase within the 4.5-hour treatment window
Identify large vessel occlusion and consult for mechanical thrombectomy
Obtain ECG to identify atrial fibrillation as the stroke mechanism
Initiate anticoagulation for secondary prevention once safe to do so
Monitor in ICU/stroke unit with serial neurological assessments
contraindicated_actions: Administering anticoagulation (heparin, DOAC) in the acute phase before excluding hemorrhagic transformation
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute phase (risk of worsening ischemia)
Administering antiplatelet agents concurrently with IV alteplase within 24 hours
Delaying IV alteplase to obtain MRI in a patient within the treatment window
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of acute large territory stroke with mass effect
Administering IV alteplase if INR were elevated above 1.7
key_reasoning_points: Sudden onset focal neurological deficit (right hemiparesis, global aphasia) with last known well within 4.5 hours defines an acute stroke presentation eligible for IV thrombolysis
Known atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation is the most likely stroke mechanism (cardioembolic), supported by ECG showing AF with RVR
MRI demonstrates classic acute ischemic stroke pattern: DWI hyperintensity with ADC hypointensity in a vascular territory (left MCA) with corresponding MRA showing M1 occlusion
Large vessel occlusion (left MCA M1) mandates consideration of mechanical thrombectomy in addition to IV thrombolysis (bridging therapy)
Normal INR confirms the patient is not anticoagulated, making him eligible for IV alteplase and reinforcing the history of non-adherence to recommended anticoagulation
Multiple modifiable risk factors (AF, hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, former smoking) compound stroke risk and must be addressed for secondary prevention
NIHSS of 18 indicates a severe stroke; combined with LVO, this patient benefits most from combined IV tPA and mechanical thrombectomy

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic presentation with sudden onset right hemiparesis and aphasia, known AF not on anticoagulation, clear LVO on imaging
clinical_pearls: Time is brain: every minute of delayed reperfusion results in loss of approximately 1.9 million neurons
AF is the most common cause of cardioembolic stroke, and patients with AF-related stroke have higher recurrence rates without anticoagulation
The combination of IV tPA and mechanical thrombectomy (bridging therapy) is superior to IV tPA alone for LVO
